local_names.romanized_local: Bai Zhu Dan (Chinese); Telur Rebus (Malay); Ubla hua anda (Hindi romanized)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of hard-boiled eggs in Singapore is 2 medium-sized eggs (approximately 100g), commonly served as a side dish alongside rice or curry.

nutrient_source.title: Egg, whole, cooked, hard-boiled — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, FDC ID 173424
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for hard-boiled whole egg (FDC ID 173424) used as primary nutrient reference. This is an approved secondary global source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. Values cross-checked against Singapore HPB Foodo database for boiled egg. Input values from the GrabFood record were consistent with USDA data and have been retained where aligned. Saturated fat updated from input value of 3.5g to 3.3g to align with USDA SR Legacy data.

gi_source.title: University of Sydney GI Database — eggs not listed; GI not applicable for negligible-carbohydrate foods
gi_source.source_match_notes: Hard-boiled egg contains only 1.1g carbohydrate per 100g. The University of Sydney GI Database does not assign a GI value to eggs as they contain negligible carbohydrates and do not produce a measurable glycaemic response. Per Veritas protocol for negligible-carbohydrate foods (eggs, pure proteins, pure fats), glycemic_index is set to null (not 0) and gi_category is set to not_applicable. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate contribution is negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 124mg/100g — below the 200mg/100g threshold; no added salt in plain boiling
hypertension.guidance: Hard-boiled eggs are suitable for hypertensive individuals when consumed plain without added salt or high-sodium condiments, as sodium content at 124mg/100g is well within safe limits; limit to 1–2 eggs per day and avoid pairing with soy sauce or ketchup to maintain low sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (1.1g/100g) and high protein (13.0g/100g) support glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Hard-boiled eggs have a negligible glycaemic impact due to minimal carbohydrate content (1.1g/100g) and are an excellent protein source (13.0g/100g) that promotes satiety and slows gastric emptying; individuals with type 2 diabetes and concurrent dyslipidaemia should limit intake to 1–2 eggs per day given the high cholesterol content (372mg/100g).

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary cholesterol (372mg/100g) and moderate saturated fat (3.3g/100g) warrant moderation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While saturated fat is moderate (3.3g/100g) and eggs provide beneficial nutrients including vitamin D and B12, the high cholesterol content (372mg/100g) means individuals with established cardiovascular disease or hypercholesterolaemia should limit consumption to no more than 1 egg per day as part of a heart-healthy diet, in line with current clinical guidance.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A hard-boiled egg (also called a full boil egg in Singapore hawker and Indian food contexts) is a whole hen's egg cooked by immersion in boiling water until both the white (albumen) and yolk are fully set and firm. The cooking process requires approximately 9–12 minutes in simmering water. The result is a firm, slightly rubbery white and a dense, crumbly, pale-yellow yolk with a mild, savoury flavour. In Singapore, hard-boiled eggs are commonly served as a side dish alongside Indian curry rice, nasi lemak, or as a protein accompaniment in mixed rice (economy rice) stalls. They are also a staple protein in local Indian-Muslim and South Indian cuisine contexts. Nutritionally, hard-boiled eggs are an excellent source of complete protein (13.0g/100g), providing all essential amino acids. They contain moderate fat (11.0g/100g) with a favourable unsaturated-to-saturated ratio, and are rich in micronutrients including vitamin B12 (1.11mcg/100g), vitamin A (149mcg RAE/100g), vitamin D (2.0mcg/100g), selenium (30.8mcg/100g), and phosphorus (172mg/100g). Carbohydrate content is negligible (1.1g/100g), making them suitable for low-carbohydrate and diabetic dietary patterns. The high cholesterol content (372mg/100g) warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia. Boiling is the lowest-AGE cooking method for eggs, making it preferable to frying for individuals with inflammatory conditions.
common_user_queries: Is a hard-boiled egg good for diabetes?
How many calories in a boiled egg?
Is boiled egg good for high blood pressure?
Can I eat boiled eggs if I have heart disease?
What is the protein content of a hard-boiled egg?
How much sodium in a boiled egg?

Values are consistent with Singapore HPB Foodo database entries for boiled egg. All mandatory fields are complete and all hard gates pass.
notes: Dietary fibre is 0.0g/100g — naturally zero as eggs are an animal product containing no plant-based fibre; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 173424. Vitamin C is 0.0mg/100g — naturally zero as eggs contain no ascorbic acid; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. Trans fat is 0.0g/100g — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for plain boiled egg. Saturated fat updated from input value of 3.5g to 3.3g to align with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data (FDC ID 173424). Monounsaturated fat (4.1g/100g), polyunsaturated fat (1.4g/100g), and water (74.6g/100g) populated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. All micronutrient values (vitamins A, B1, B2, B3, B6, B12, D, E, folate, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, potassium) populated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 173424. Hard-boiled egg; glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (1.1g/100g); glycemic_index set to null and gi_category set to not_applicable per Veritas protocol for negligible-carbohydrate foods. The input record described the dish as Indian cuisine context served as part of Indian meals in Singapore; multilingual names provided for all five mandatory Singaporean cuisine languages: English, Chinese Simplified (白煮蛋), Chinese Traditional (白煮蛋), Malay (Telur Rebus), Tamil (வேகவைத்த முட்டை), and Hindi (उबला हुआ अंडा). Input health_suitability for rheumatoid_arthritis noted suitable=false with contradictory reasoning; this condition has been excluded from health_context as it is not one of the three mandatory conditions and the clinical reasoning was internally inconsistent. The three mandatory health conditions (Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, Cardiovascular Disease) are fully populated. Nutrient source is USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (approved secondary global source per Project Veritas v2 source policy); GrabFood is not used as a nutrient source.

Verified all per_100g values are numeric with no null or string values. Confirmed g2 compliance by ensuring nutrient_source organization is USDA ARS only.
